bd_articles.title: Ramos Law buys southeast Denver office for $3M
bd_articles.author: Matt Geiger
bd_articles.summary: “Our business is just really booming down south,” Ramos said.
bd_articles.content_preview:
The building was most recently a bank branch. (Courtesy of Ramos Law)
Attorney Joe Ramos doesn’t have a downtown corner office.
But in southeast Denver, he’ll have an office on a corner.
The head of personal injury firm Ramos Law purchased a 15,600-square-foot building at the corner of Hampden Avenue and Monaco Street in Denver last week for $3 million from UMB Bank. The deal works out to $189 a square foot.
Ramos Law will consolidate offices in east Denver and Centennial, both leased, into the

bd_articles.title: Hotel, credit union next to Central Park homeless shelter find a buyer
bd_articles.author: Thomas Gounley
bd_articles.summary: “It’s really affected all the properties in the vicinity,” a broker said of the shelter.
bd_articles.content_preview:
The former Courtyard by Marriott hotel at 7415 E. 41st Ave. in Denver on March 3, 2026. (Thomas Gounley/BusinessDen)
A credit union and hotel that flank a homeless shelter at the edge of Denver’s Central Park neighborhood have sold to the same buyer.
Aurora-based 2nd Home Community, a provider of adult day services and home care, purchased the former Partner Colorado Credit Union building at 4000 Quebec St. in January for $800,000, according to public records.
And on Friday, the company bought t

bd_articles.title: Former Punch Bowl Social exec succeeds Padró as CEO of restaurant group
bd_articles.author: Max Scheinblum
bd_articles.summary: The announcement comes after settlement of a lawsuit against Culinary Creative Group, which plans to pursue a “clustered growth” strategy.
bd_articles.content_preview:
Culinary Creative Group co-founder Juan Padro makes an announcement about food during the 9th annual annual orphans’ Christmas Wednesday, Dec. 25, 2019 at Highland Tap & Burger. The restaurant group announced Padro would step down as CEO of the company Wednesday, Feb. 25, 2026. (Daniel Brenner/Denver Post)
After 15 years at the helm of Culinary Creative Group, Juan Padró is leaving the kitchen.
“I’m a kid from Worcester. I’m an entrepreneur,” the Massachusetts native said. “I’m not this strategi

bd_articles.title: Modular builder Fading West demands millions from developer of Granby project
bd_articles.author: Justin Wingerter
bd_articles.summary: Fading West was told by Summit Housing to stop work on a $10 million order in October.
bd_articles.content_preview:
Fading West’s 110,000-square-foot manufacturing facility in Buena Vista. (Colorado Housing and Finance Authority)
A state-backed manufacturer of modular homes is seeking $5.7 million from a developer after that developer ordered it to stop work on a modular apartment project in Granby.
Fading West, which has been making modular buildings in Buena Vista since 2016, is asking an arbitrator to make Summit Housing Group, a Montana-based developer, pay up. Modular homes are created in a warehouse and

bd_articles.title: Fentress sells Broadway buildings to Magnetic for $13M
bd_articles.author: Thomas Gounley
bd_articles.summary: The architect behind DIA provided seller financing. The deal was once slated to be much bigger.
bd_articles.content_preview:
The Fentress Studios building at 421 Broadway in Denver on March 2, 2026. (Thomas Gounley/BusinessDen)
Architect Curtis Fentress has sold two buildings along Broadway in Denver, including one where his firm has long operated.
The founder of Fentress Architects, which was bought by Populous last year and is now known as Fentress Studios, sold 421 and 433 Broadway on Friday for $12.75 million, according to public records. The deal works out to $253 a square foot for the 1.15-acre site.
Curtis Fent

bd_articles.title: LoHi hotel being demolished ahead of apartment project
bd_articles.author: Thomas Gounley
bd_articles.summary: The former Residence Inn property along Zuni sold in December.
bd_articles.content_preview:
American Demolition has begun knocking down the former Residence Inn in LoHi. (Courtesy American Demolition)
A sprawling LoHi hotel is coming down.
Crews from American Demolition are knocking down the former Residence Inn at 2777 Zuni St. in Denver. 
The hotel, made up of a host of two-story buildings across 4 acres, was purchased in December by San Antonio, Texas-based Kairoi Residential. The company paid $39 million, or $231 a square foot for the land.
Development plans show Kairoi plans to bu

bd_articles.title: Former Avs defenseman launches beer brand in Denver
bd_articles.author: Max Scheinblum
bd_articles.summary: Tyson Barrie’s Chilly Ones are a 3% light lager and a soon-to-debut nonalcoholic version, plus a 4.5% to 5% option that's brewing.
bd_articles.content_preview:
From left: Chilly Ones co-founder and “Chief Chilly One” Tyson Barrie, brand ambassador and Avs star Nathan MacKinnon, COO Kimberley Kainth and CEO Matthew Clayton. (Courtesy Chilly Ones)
While most people know beers as “cold ones,” Tyson Barrie opts for a different name.
“We’ve always just called beers chilly ones,” the former Colorado Avalanche defenseman said.
Now, Barrie hopes his moniker goes mainstream with his beer brand Chilly Ones, which made its U.S. debut weeks ago in Colorado. He pla

bd_articles.title: Luzzatto clears ground lease hurdle for $300M downtown project
bd_articles.author: Matt Geiger
bd_articles.summary: His planned conversion of 633 and 621 17th St. had been hamstrung by a tiny ground lease under one of the buildings.
bd_articles.content_preview:
The office towers at 621 17th St., right, and 633 17th St. as seen from the corner of 17th and California streets on Nov. 19, 2024. (BusinessDen file)
Asher Luzzatto’s downtown office-to-residential conversion has new life after six months in purgatory.
“There were definitely a lot of moments where I wasn’t sure it was going to happen,” said Luzzatto, 38. 
On Friday, Luzzatto and Rhoda Krasner agreed to extend a ground lease expiration from 29 to 99 years at 633 17th St. The length of the lease

bd_articles.title: Chicago firm mismanaged project before abandoning it, RiNo developer says
bd_articles.author: Justin Wingerter
bd_articles.summary: “John Buck Co.’s spending bore no resemblance to the project budget,” Menalto says, citing $10 million in spending without a groundbreaking.
bd_articles.content_preview:
A rendering of the first phase of Hurley Place, to be constructed between the former Blue Moon Brewing and Ironton Distillery buildings. (Courtesy Menalto Development)
The RiNo firm Menalto Development is accusing a Chicago company of burning through more than $10 million “before putting a single shovel in the ground” at their project along Delgany Street, then stopping work and ceasing communication about it.
“Menalto is entitled to recover damages the John Buck Co. has caused Menalto and the f

bd_articles.title: Restaurant industry report calls for dropping Denver tipped wage to state minimum
bd_articles.author: Max Scheinblum
bd_articles.summary: Councilman Darrell Watson said Friday he plans to propose changes, although not that drastic.
bd_articles.content_preview:
Alexis Apodaca Fresquez brings coffee to tables for refills during the breakfast rush at Sam’s No. 3 diner in downtown Denver on Aug. 28, 2024. (RJ Sangosti/The Denver Post)
Increased labor costs are ​​“the most frequently cited challenge” among restaurant owners and the “most destabilizing factor” for Denver’s full-service establishments.
That’s according to a 67-page industry-led report released Friday, in which veteran Colorado restaurateurs Dana Faulk Query and Adam Schlegel detailed the cit

bd_articles.title: Golf-centric coworking business adding second location
bd_articles.author: Matt Geiger
bd_articles.summary: Within the first 13 months of opening, Josh Kaplan and Joe Skovgaard have reached 100% occupancy in Greenwood Village.
bd_articles.content_preview:
Joe Skovgaard, left, launched Clubhouse Work & Golf with Josh Kaplan in late 2024. (Matt Geiger/BusinessDen)
Josh Kaplan and Joe Skovgaard have set a tee time for an office building on Colorado Boulevard come late summer. 
The two entrepreneurs are opening a second location of Clubhouse Work & Golf, a coworking space with golf simulators, in 24,000 square feet at 720 S. Colorado Blvd. Most of that will be offices on the top floor of the building’s north tower, with space for five Trackman golf s

bd_articles.title: After a rocky ownership run, Crescent looks to sell the Brown Palace
bd_articles.author: Thomas Gounley
bd_articles.summary: “Our goal is to find a good buyer who is well capitalized, who appreciates the legacy and wants to take it to the next level.”
bd_articles.content_preview:
People pass by empty windows at the iconic Brown Palace Hotel in Denver on Sept. 4, 2024. (Helen H. Richardson/The Denver Post)
The Brown Palace Hotel, one of downtown’s most iconic buildings, is on the market.
Newmark has been tapped to market the 9-story, 243-room hotel at 321 17th St., which has been owned since 2018 by Crescent Real Estate. The Brown Palace opened in August 1892, making it Denver’s second-oldest hotel still in operation, behind the Oxford Hotel in LoDo.
A deal would also inc

daily.text:
BusinessDen received significant pickup across Denver media outlets today, with **The Denver Post** leading coverage by referencing six separate BusinessDen stories. The Post covered a diverse range of business news including Fentress's $13M sale of Broadway buildings to Magnetic Capital, a dispute between modular builder Fading West and a Granby project developer, a coworking space with golf simulators expanding to a second Denver location, Luzzatto's progress on a $300M downtown office-to-residential conversion, former Avalanche defenseman launching a Denver beer brand, and Ramos Law's $3M southeast Denver office purchase.

The **Brown Palace Hotel sale story** generated the most widespread attention, getting picked up by multiple major outlets including **Denverite**, **9News**, **Denver Gazette**, and **Colorado Springs Gazette**. This story also sparked social media discussion on Instagram, with posts detailing how Crescent Real Estate has retained Newmark to market both the Brown Palace and adjacent Holiday Inn Express. The broad coverage suggests this is being treated as a significant local business story given the Brown Palace's historic importance to Denver.

**Hoodline** provided three separate articles referencing BusinessDen content, covering the Fentress Broadway building sale, issues with sewer problems affecting Lowry Town Center retail, and analysis of big banks gaining market share as Colorado lenders disappear. Additional mentions appeared on Facebook regarding Denver firms buying Bay Area hotels, and various Instagram posts promoted related content like Woody Creek Distillers' Colorado Avalanche bourbon.

While the mentions don't specifically identify which BusinessDen reporters authored the original stories being referenced, the breadth of coverage across real estate transactions, legal disputes, hospitality, and banking suggests multiple reporters' work was featured. The pattern shows BusinessDen serving as a key source for Denver business intelligence, with both mainstream media and digital outlets regularly amplifying their reporting across diverse commercial real estate and business sectors.

byline.text:
**Matt Geiger**

One of Geiger's three recent articles generated pickup from The Denver Post, with his commercial real estate coverage of Ramos Law's $3 million southeast Denver office purchase drawing attention from the city's largest newspaper. This represents a solid 33% pickup rate for his recent work, indicating that his commercial real estate beat continues to resonate with other outlets looking for significant property transactions in the Denver market.

**Thomas Gounley**

Gounley achieved external coverage with one of his four recent articles, as The Denver Post picked up his report on Fentress selling Broadway buildings to Magnetic for $13 million. His commercial real estate reporting appears to be hitting the right notes for larger outlets, particularly when covering substantial property deals involving established Denver firms. The pickup suggests that transactions in the $10+ million range on prominent corridors like Broadway continue to be of interest to mainstream media.

**Justin Wingerter**

Despite publishing only two articles recently, Wingerter managed to generate pickup from The Denver Post with his coverage of the legal dispute between modular builder Fading West and a Granby project developer. His story about the multi-million dollar demand represents the type of business litigation coverage that tends to attract attention from larger outlets, particularly when it involves construction disputes and significant financial stakes. This suggests that conflict-driven business stories, especially those involving real estate development, continue to have strong appeal for external coverage.
